section.title: A Light to the Nations
section.sectionRationale: This chapter contains the second of Isaiah's Servant Songs, verses 1 to 6, in which the Servant, called and named before birth, is told that restoring Jacob alone is too small a task, He will also be a light to the nations, bringing salvation to the ends of the earth. The chapter then turns to comfort Zion directly, using the tenderest image in the whole book, a mother's love, to promise that God has not forgotten His people.
section.thesis: The Servant, called from the womb for a mission that seemed to fail, is told that His calling is too great to be limited to restoring Israel alone, He will be a light to the nations, and the LORD reassures grieving Zion that, sooner than a mother could forget her nursing child, He has not and will not forget His people.
section.frameNote: Eight movements: the Servant called and named from the womb; the climactic widening of His mission to be a light for the nations; the LORD's help in the day of salvation; a highway home through every mountain made a road; the LORD's tender reassurance that He has not forgotten Zion; the sudden gathering of returning children; foreign kings serving as caretakers; and the closing certainty that the LORD will rescue His people from even the mightiest oppressor.

units.label: The LORD called Me from the womb

verses.text: Listen to Me, O islands; pay attention, O distant peoples: The LORD called Me from the womb; from the body of My mother He named Me.
He made My mouth like a sharp sword; He hid Me in the shadow of His hand. He made Me like a polished arrow; He hid Me in His quiver.
He said to Me, “You are My Servant, Israel, in whom I will display My glory.”
But I said, “I have labored in vain, I have spent My strength in futility and vanity; yet My vindication is with the LORD, and My reward is with My God.”

units.label: A light for the nations
units.note: salvation to the ends of the earth

verses.text: And now says the LORD, who formed Me from the womb to be His Servant, to bring Jacob back to Him, that Israel might be gathered to Him, for I am honored in the sight of the LORD, and My God is My strength,

He says: “It is not enough for You to be My Servant, to raise up the tribes of Jacob, and to restore the protected ones of Israel. I will also make You a {{or|light}} for the nations, to bring My salvation to the ends of the earth.”

Thus says the LORD, the Redeemer and Holy One of Israel, to Him who was despised and abhorred by the nation, to the Servant of rulers: “Kings will see You and rise, and princes will bow down, because of the LORD, who is faithful, the Holy One of Israel, who has chosen You.”

units.label: In the day of salvation I will help you

verses.text: This is what the LORD says: “In the time of favor I will answer You, and in the day of salvation I will help You; I will keep You and appoint You to be a covenant for the people, to restore the land, to apportion its desolate inheritances,
to say to the prisoners, ‘Come out,’ and to those in darkness, ‘Show yourselves.’ They will feed along the pathways, and find pasture on every barren hill.
They will not hunger or thirst, nor will scorching heat or sun beat down on them. For He who has compassion on them will guide them and lead them beside springs of water.
I will turn all My mountains into roads, and My highways will be raised up.
Behold, they will come from far away, from the north and from the west, and from the land of Aswan.”
Shout for joy, O heavens; rejoice, O earth; break forth in song, O mountains! For the LORD has comforted His people, and He will have compassion on His afflicted ones.

units.label: Can a woman forget her nursing child?

verses.text: But Zion said, “The LORD has forsaken me; the Lord has forgotten me!”
“Can a woman forget her nursing child, or lack {{racham|compassion}} for the son of her womb? Even if she could forget, I will not forget you!
Behold, I have inscribed you on the palms of My hands; your walls are ever before Me.

units.label: Lift up your eyes and look around

verses.text: Your builders hasten back; your destroyers and wreckers depart from you.
Lift up your eyes and look around. They all gather together; they come to you. As surely as I live,” declares the LORD, “you will wear them all as jewelry and put them on like a bride.
For your ruined and desolate places and your ravaged land will now indeed be too small for your people, and those who devoured you will be far away.
Yet the children of your bereavement will say in your hearing, ‘This place is too small for us; make room for us to live here.’

Then you will say in your heart, ‘Who has begotten these for me? I was bereaved and barren; I was exiled and rejected. So who has reared them? Look, I was left all alone, so where did they come from?’”

units.label: Kings will be your foster fathers

verses.text: This is what the Lord GOD says: “Behold, I will lift up My hand to the nations, and raise My banner to the peoples. They will bring your sons in their arms and carry your daughters on their shoulders.

Kings will be your foster fathers, and their queens your nursing mothers. They will bow to you facedown and lick the dust at your feet. Then you will know that I am the LORD; those who hope in Me will never be put to shame.”

units.label: Can the plunder be snatched from the mighty?
units.note: all flesh will know their Savior

verses.text: Can the plunder be snatched from the mighty, or the captives of a tyrant be delivered?

Indeed, this is what the LORD says: “Even the captives of the mighty will be taken away, and the plunder of the tyrant will be retrieved; I will contend with those who contend with you, and I will save your children.

I will make your oppressors eat their own flesh; they will be drunk on their own blood, as with wine. Then all mankind will know that I, the LORD, am your Savior and your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob.”

or.gloss: Light, the very first thing God calls into being in Genesis 1:3. To be made a light for the nations is to be given the highest possible calling, bringing not merely information but salvation itself to peoples far beyond Israel, a mission the New Testament identifies as fulfilled and continued in Christ and His church.

racham.gloss: Compassion, tender mercy, a word built on the same root as rechem, womb, so that a mother's compassion for the child of her own body becomes the very picture Scripture reaches for to describe God's love. Even this deepest human bond, the LORD insists, is a weaker analogy than His own unforgettable commitment to His people.

basic.context: This chapter contains the second Servant Song, where a figure called and named before birth confesses that His labor has felt like failure, yet is told that His mission is actually too great to be limited to restoring Israel, He will be a light to the nations, bringing salvation to the ends of the earth. The chapter then turns to comfort a grieving Zion, who feels forgotten, with the tenderest image available: a mother's love for her own nursing child.
basic.hermeneutics: The Servant is called 'Israel' in verse 3 yet is given a mission to Israel in verses 5 to 6, restoring Jacob and gathering Israel, a tension resolved when the Servant is understood to represent and embody Israel's true calling even while personally distinct from the nation, succeeding at the very vocation Israel as a whole had failed. This reading is confirmed directly when Paul and Barnabas apply verse 6 to their own Gentile mission grounded in Christ, Acts 13:47.
basic.originalLanguages: The Servant's mission climaxes in being made an {{or}}, light, for the nations, the highest possible calling, bringing salvation itself to the ends of the earth. Zion's despair is answered by an appeal to {{racham}}, compassion, the tender mercy a mother feels for the child of her own womb, which the LORD says is a weaker bond than His own unbreakable commitment to His people.
basic.typology: The Servant called and named from the womb, whose labor initially seems futile yet is vindicated and expanded into a mission to the whole earth, anticipates Christ's own path through apparent failure at the cross to global, resurrection-vindicated triumph. The image of Zion's children being restored to her, more numerous than she can hold, anticipates the growth of the church gathered from every nation.
basic.christ: Paul and Barnabas quote Isaiah 49:6 directly, declaring, 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth,' and apply this Servant's mission to their own gospel preaching, grounded in Christ, Acts 13:47. Simeon likewise recognizes the infant Jesus as 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles,' Luke 2:32, directly echoing this Servant Song. The Servant's confession, 'I have labored in vain,' verse 4, anticipates and is answered by Christ's resurrection, which vindicated what looked, at the cross, like total failure.
basic.god: God calls and names His Servant before birth for a purpose far greater than first appears, and He does not abandon that purpose even when it seems to fail. He is also the God whose compassion for His people exceeds even a mother's love for her nursing child, a bond He Himself created as merely a faint echo of His own unbreakable commitment.
basic.crossReferences: Acts 13:47 quotes Isaiah 49:6 directly, applying the Servant's mission of light to the nations to the apostolic proclamation of Christ. Luke 2:32 has Simeon recognize the infant Jesus as this very light for revelation to the Gentiles. John 10:28-29 echoes the promise that no one can snatch God's people from His hand. Matthew 2:15 applies similar Israel-and-Servant typology, quoting Hosea 11:1 of the infant Jesus.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's Servant, whose apparent failure becomes the platform for a mission to the whole earth, and its picture of a God whose compassion exceeds even a mother's love, traces the whole Bible's pattern of purpose vindicated through weakness and love that will not let go. It anticipates Christ, the true Light to the nations, who was despised yet is now exalted, gathering children for God from every corner of the earth.

context.literary: Structured in eight movements around the second Servant Song, verses 1 to 7, followed by an extended promise of restoration and comfort for Zion, moving from apparent failure to triumphant, tender reassurance and closing with certain deliverance from even the mightiest oppressor.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles who needed both the Servant's mission clarified, restoring Israel is not the limit of God's redemptive purpose, and Zion's own grief answered directly, since many genuinely felt abandoned during the long years of captivity.
context.canonical: The second of Isaiah's four Servant Songs, directly quoted in Acts 13:47 as fulfilled in the apostolic mission grounded in Christ, and its themes of comfort and restoration continue the Book of Comfort's argument through to its close in chapter 55.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal that the Servant's calling, though it may feel like failure, is actually too great to be confined to restoring Israel alone, extending to salvation for the whole earth, and to reassure grieving Zion with the deepest human image of love that God has not forgotten her.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes the Servant's specific calling and God's specific comfort to exiled Zion; it is not a general guarantee that every faithful servant's apparent failure will be publicly reversed in the same visible way. Its abiding principle is that God's purposes often triumph through what looks like failure, and that His compassion for His people is more certain than any human bond.

debates.question: Why is the Servant called 'Israel' in verse 3 when He is also given a mission to Israel in verses 5 to 6?

views.label: The Servant represents and embodies Israel's true calling
views.case: The Servant can be called 'Israel' because He recapitulates and fulfills, in His own person, the vocation the nation Israel was given but failed to keep, so that restoring the actual nation becomes part of His mission precisely because He stands in Israel's place, similar to how Matthew applies Hosea's language about Israel, 'out of Egypt I called my son,' directly to the infant Jesus, Matthew 2:15.

views.label: The title reflects an early collective identification later distinguished from an individual figure
views.case: Some interpreters note that a few ancient manuscript traditions do not include 'Israel' in verse 3, suggesting the term may reflect an early collective reading of the Servant that the surrounding verses, distinguishing the Servant's mission to Israel, ultimately move beyond toward an individual figure.

debates.bearing: However the tension is resolved historically, the New Testament's direct application of this Servant Song to Christ and to the gospel mission, Acts 13:47, confirms that the individual Messiah is the Servant in whom Israel's true vocation, embodying and extending God's light to the nations, is finally and fully realized.

study.originalLanguages: The Servant's mission reaches its climax in being made {{or}}, light, for the nations, carrying salvation to the ends of the earth, the highest calling given in these songs. Zion's grief is answered by appeal to {{racham}}, compassion, the same root as the word for womb, so that the tenderest human bond becomes only a faint shadow of God's own unbreakable love for His people.

typology.type: The Servant whose apparent failure is vindicated and expanded into a worldwide mission
typology.fulfillment: Christ, whose apparent defeat at the cross became, through resurrection, the foundation for salvation reaching the whole earth.

typology.type: Zion's children gathered home, more numerous than she can hold
typology.fulfillment: The church, gathered from every nation as God's people through Christ.

study.christ: Paul and Barnabas quote this Servant Song directly, applying verse 6, 'I have made you a light for the Gentiles, that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth,' to their own gospel mission grounded entirely in Christ, Acts 13:47. Simeon likewise recognizes the infant Jesus as this very promised 'light for revelation to the Gentiles,' Luke 2:32. The Servant's own confession that His labor felt like failure, verse 4, is answered and reversed at the resurrection, which vindicated what appeared at the cross to be total defeat.
study.god: God calls and names His Servant before birth for a purpose that outlasts every appearance of failure, and He does not abandon that purpose even when the Servant Himself feels its futility. He is also the God whose compassion for His people surpasses even a mother's bond with her own nursing child, a bond He created as merely a faint echo of His own unbreakable commitment to remember and restore His people.

crossReferences.note: Quotes Isaiah 49:6 directly, applying the Servant's mission of light to the nations to the apostolic proclamation of the gospel.
Simeon recognizes the infant Jesus as the light for revelation to the Gentiles promised in this chapter.
Echoes the promise that no one can snatch God's people from His hand, the same assurance given in verses 24 to 25.
Applies similar Israel-and-Servant typology to the infant Jesus, quoting Hosea 11:1.

study.oneStory: This chapter's Servant, whose apparent failure becomes the platform for a mission reaching the whole earth, and its picture of a God whose compassion exceeds even a mother's love, traces the whole Bible's pattern of purpose vindicated through weakness and a love that will not let go. It anticipates Christ, the true Light to the nations, once despised yet now exalted, gathering children for God from every corner of the earth.

quiz.q: What confession does the Servant make in verse 4 despite His high calling?

options.text: That He has labored in vain and spent His strength in futility, yet trusts His vindication is with the LORD.
options.note: Verse 4 records exactly this honest confession alongside continued trust in God.

options.text: That His mission has been an unqualified, visible success from the start.
options.note: The opposite; the Servant candidly confesses a sense of futility and failure.

options.text: That He no longer trusts God at all.
options.note: Despite the sense of futility, the Servant explicitly states His vindication and reward remain with the LORD.

options.text: That He was never truly called by God in the first place.
options.note: The Servant's confession assumes and reaffirms His calling; His concern is the apparent fruit of His labor, not the reality of His calling.

quiz.q: What does the LORD say is 'not enough' for the Servant's mission in verse 6?

options.text: Restoring Jacob and the tribes of Israel alone; He will also be a light to the nations.
options.note: Verse 6 states this expansion of mission directly.

options.text: Saving anyone at all, since the mission is declared impossible.
options.note: The opposite; the verse expands rather than cancels the Servant's saving mission.

options.text: Speaking to kings and princes, since only common people matter.
options.note: Verse 7 actually describes kings rising and princes bowing, not an exclusion of rulers from the mission's scope.

options.text: Working through prophets, since only priests may act.
options.note: The passage does not distinguish between prophets and priests; it expands the Servant's mission to encompass the nations broadly.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament apply Isaiah 49:6?

options.text: Paul and Barnabas quote it directly as grounds for their mission bringing salvation to the Gentiles.
options.note: Acts 13:47 quotes this verse directly and applies it to the apostolic gospel mission.

options.text: It is never referenced anywhere in the New Testament.
options.note: The opposite; it is quoted directly in Acts 13:47 and echoed in Luke 2:32.

options.text: It is applied only to a future, entirely unrelated event with no Gentile mission connection.
options.note: Its clearest New Testament use directly connects it to the Gentile mission grounded in Christ.

options.text: It is used to argue against including Gentiles in salvation.
options.note: The opposite; it is used specifically to justify and ground the inclusion of Gentiles in the gospel mission.

quiz.q: What image does the LORD use in verse 15 to reassure grieving Zion?

options.text: A mother's inability to forget her own nursing child, though God's love exceeds even that.
options.note: Verse 15 uses exactly this image, then surpasses it with God's unbreakable commitment.

options.text: A soldier's duty to his commanding officer.
options.note: The image used is maternal love and compassion, not military duty.

options.text: A business contract between two merchants.
options.note: The passage uses intimate, familial imagery, not transactional business language.

options.text: A king's obligation to his most distant subjects.
options.note: The specific image given is a mother's bond with her nursing child, a far more intimate picture than a king's general obligations.

quiz.q: What certainty does the LORD express in verses 24 to 26 regarding Israel's rescue?

options.text: Even the captives of the mighty will be taken away, for the LORD Himself will contend with their oppressors.
options.note: Verses 24 to 25 state this certainty directly, that the LORD will rescue even from the mightiest tyrant.

options.text: Israel's rescue is uncertain and depends entirely on human military strength.
options.note: The opposite; the passage grounds the certainty of rescue in the LORD's own action, not human military might.

options.text: Only the wealthy among the captives will be rescued.
options.note: The promise is given broadly to God's people and their children, not selectively to the wealthy.

options.text: Rescue will occur, but only after many centuries of continued captivity.
options.note: The passage emphasizes the LORD's active, certain intervention, not an indefinite, centuries-long delay.

questions: The Servant confesses His labor felt like failure, yet trusts His vindication is with the LORD. Where does your own work or ministry feel fruitless right now, and how might you need to entrust the outcome to God rather than your own sense of success?
The Servant's mission was expanded far beyond what seemed 'enough,' restoring Israel, to being a light to the whole world. Where might God be calling you to a bigger vision than you have settled for?
Zion felt forgotten by God even while He carried her on the palms of His hands. When have you felt forgotten by God in a season that, looking back, you can now see He was present in all along?
God's compassion is compared to, and said to exceed, a mother's love for her nursing child. How does picturing God's love in this tender, maternal way change how you approach Him today?

The chapter closes with certainty that God will rescue His people even from the mightiest oppressor. What situation in your life feels too powerful or entrenched to change, and how might this promise reshape your prayers about it?

translationNotes.note: This is the second of Isaiah's four Servant Songs, directly quoted in Acts 13:47 and echoed in Luke 2:32 regarding the mission of light to the nations.

The Servant is called 'Israel' here even while being given a mission to restore Israel in the following verses, a tension most naturally resolved by understanding the Servant as embodying and fulfilling Israel's true vocation representatively.
The land of Aswan, sometimes rendered Sinim in other translations, likely refers to a distant southern region, emphasizing that the returning exiles will come from every direction, however far.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 49:1-26. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
